coredata.dc:title: Nano-enabled sensing approaches for pathogenic bacterial detection
coredata.dc:creator: Alafeef, Maha
Moitra, Parikshit
Pan, Dipanjan
coredata.dc:description: Infectious diseases caused by pathogenic bacteria, especially antibiotic-resistant bacteria, are one of the biggest threats to global health. To date, bacterial contamination is detected using conventional culturing techniques, which are highly dependent on expert users, limited by the processing time and on-site availability. Hence, real-time and continuous monitoring of pathogen levels is required to obtain valuable information that could assist health agencies in guiding prevention and containment of pathogen-related outbreaks. Nanotechnology-based smart sensors are opening new avenues for early and rapid detection of such pathogens at the patient's point-of-care. Nanomaterials can play an essential role in bacterial sensing owing to their unique optical, magnetic, and electrical properties. Carbon nanoparticles, metallic nanoparticles, metal oxide nanoparticles, and various types of nanocomposites are examples of smart nanomaterials that have drawn intense attention in the field of microbial detection. These approaches, together with the advent of modern technologies and coupled with machine learning and wireless communication, represent the future trend in the diagnosis of infectious diseases. This review provides an overview of the recent advancements in the successful harnessing of different nanoparticles for bacterial detection. In the beginning, we have introduced the fundamental concepts and mechanisms behind the design and strategies of the nanoparticles-based diagnostic platform. Representative research efforts are highlighted for in vitro and in vivo detection of bacteria. A comprehensive discussion is then presented to cover the most commonly adopted techniques for bacterial identification, including some seminal studies to detect bacteria at the single-cell level. Finally, we discuss the current challenges and a prospective outlook on the field, together with the recommended solutions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Infectious diseases caused by pathogenic bacteria, especially antibiotic-resistant bacteria, are one of the biggest threats to global health. To date, bacterial contamination is detected using conventional culturing techniques, which are highly dependent on expert users, limited by the processing time and on-site availability. Hence, real-time and continuous monitoring of pathogen levels is required to obtain valuable information that could assist health agencies in guiding prevention and containment of pathogen-related outbreaks. Nanotechnology-based smart sensors are opening new avenues for early and rapid detection of such pathogens at the patient's point-of-care. Nanomaterials can play an essential role in bacterial sensing owing to their unique optical, magnetic, and electrical properties. Carbon nanoparticles, metallic nanoparticles, metal oxide nanoparticles, and various types of nanocomposites are examples of smart nanomaterials that have drawn intense attention in the field of microbial detection. These approaches, together with the advent of modern technologies and coupled with machine learning and wireless communication, represent the future trend in the diagnosis of infectious diseases. This review provides an overview of the recent advancements in the successful harnessing of different nanoparticles for bacterial detection. In the beginning, we have introduced the fundamental concepts and mechanisms behind the design and strategies of the nanoparticles-based diagnostic platform. Representative research efforts are highlighted for in vitro and in vivo detection of bacteria. A comprehensive discussion is then presented to cover the most commonly adopted techniques for bacterial identification, including some seminal studies to detect bacteria at the single-cell level. Finally, we discuss the current challenges and a prospective outlook on the field, together with the recommended solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: The proliferation of harmful bacterial strains in vivo causes bacterial infections, which affect many people around the world (Jones et al., 2008; Lutter, 1999; Mather et al., 2013; Stokstad, 2011). Selective detection of the pathogen is, therefore, of a critical need to prevent their outbreak, which is directly associated with human diseases and a high mortality rate (van der Meer, 2013). Every year, approximately 23,000 of death cases have been recorded in the US alone due to the infection caused by different antibiotic-resistant bacteria, with two million new cases diagnosed every year (Prevention, 2013). Antibiotic-resistant bacteria are, therefore, a significant burden on the health-care system, resulting in an enormous increase in expenditure and affect the national economy by about 55–70 million dollars annually (Rehm and Bartlett, 2006; Roberts et al., 2009). This trend in infectious disease outbreak foreshadows a new global threat with an expectation of about 10 million annual death-cases worldwide by 2050 (de Kraker et al., 2016).

Food poisoning or foodborne illness is an infection affecting the gastrointestinal (GI) tract that is caused by food or beverages contaminated with harmful bacteria, parasites, viruses, or chemicals (Rohde et al., 2015; Thanh Ngo et al., 2014). An estimated 48 million people in the United States (U.S.) either get sick or suffer from complications due to foodborne illness every year (Alafeef et al., 2019a; Rohde et al., 2015). It also causes around 128,000 cases of hospitalizations and about 3000 deaths in the United States (U.S.) with an approximate annual healthcare cost of US $10–13 billion (Authority et al., 2018; Chemburu et al., 2005). While most instances go unreported to health departments and are therefore of unknown origin, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) estimates that 31 known foodborne pathogens cause around 9.4 million of the cases and that seven pathogens cause 90% of all instances: Salmonella, Norovirus, Campylobacter, Toxoplasma, Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157:H7, Listeria and Clostridium perfringens. Early detection and identification of contamination due to pathogens is a key concern since infections due to this microorganism have an enormous impact on social health and the global economy.

Lack of access to a rapid laboratory test delays the pathogen identification, consequently, leading to excess antibiotics prescriptions. The over-prescribing of antibiotics subjected the human microbiome to an unnecessary number of antibiotic agents, leading to their mutation and emerge as antibiotic-resistant strains. Form here, a critical need for developing new technology arises, which can accurately and reliably detect the presence of pathogens before the subject is rendered ill. The technologies are therefore guaranteed to provide an effective treatment to the patients (Brown et al., 2012; Cohen, 2000), helping to prevent high healthcare costs, and most importantly, save people's lives (Hussain and Dawson, 2013; SCHARFF, 2012; Zimlichman et al., 2013).

The gold-standard for pathogen detection involves conventional solid/liquid culturing techniques (Ge and Meng, 2009; Jasson et al., 2010; Leonard et al., 2003; Priyanka et al., 2016; Rohde et al., 2015). Currently, these testing techniques occur at dedicated centralized laboratories using a primarily automated analyzer. This centralized testing requires additional sample transportation, waiting time, and administration, all of which increase costs for the end-users. For example, the NF EN ISO 11290-1 method for detection of Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) requires about seven days to get results after culturing of the microorganism (Leonard et al., 2003). Overall, these methods can give qualitative and quantitative information but are restricted by assay time at locations in the food processing distribution network and require up to seven days to produce a definitive result (Leonard et al., 2003). Unfortunately, delays in outcomes are still accountable for an unlimited number of mortalities and morbidities, and therefore there is a need for faster, least expensive, and arguably decentralized methods of detection. During the past few years, a lot of efficient methods such as quartz crystal microbalance (QCM), colorimetry, polymerase chain reaction (PCR), fluorescence, electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), time-resolved fluorescence assay, chemiluminescence immunoassay, bioluminescent immunoassay, and electrochemical immunoassay have been developed for the detection of pathogens based on immunoreaction (Chen et al., 2016; Farka et al., 2014; Garrido et al., 2013; Kawasaki et al., 2010; Li et al., 2011, 2015b; Pirinçci et al., 2018; Shih et al., 2015; Su et al., 2013; Thanh Ngo et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2009; Yang et al., 2012; Yang and Li 2006). Most of these techniques suffer from being non-selective, tedious and expensive or both. Moreover, these are subjective tests, and interpretation of the response is limited based on access to the trained specialists, and availability of specialty equipment (computer and software). Further, the detection of biofilm formation is challenging using these approaches (Xu et al., 2020b).

To circumvent the shortage of conventional techniques, various peptides (Kulagina et al., 2005), functionalized polymers (Disney et al., 2004), graphene-based polymer composites (Khan et al., 2018), CRISPR-based probes (Shen et al., 2020) and nanomaterials have been harnessed for bacterial detection (Chen et al. 2017, 2018b; Hao et al., 2016; Rajapaksha et al., 2019; Verma et al., 2016; Westmeier et al., 2018; Whiteley et al., 2017). Among these materials, nanoparticles (NPs), have garnered considerable attention as a building block for biosensor (Mustafa et al., 2017) development and as nanoprobe for in vivo imaging to diagnose the bacterial infection (Ostadhossein et al., 2018; Tang et al., 2019). Nanoparticles include carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), metallic nanoparticles, metal oxide nanoparticles, and various kinds of nanocomposites, all of which are excellent candidates that have been used to design bacterial-responsive nanoprobes. This is owing to their well-suited characteristics, such as large surface area, controllable morphology, unique and tunable optical properties, and exceptional mechanical, electromagnetic, and chemical properties (Alafeef et al., 2019a; Baker and Baker, 2010; Chen et al., 2008; Fathi et al., 2019; Lane et al., 2015; Misra et al. 2015, 2016, 2017; Qian et al., 2012; Smith and Nie, 2010; Srivastava et al., 2019). Among the various types of nanoparticles, CNPs have evolved as an emerging class of NPs that has gained considerable attention over the past few decades for designing imaging and sensing agents. That is owing to their photobleaching resistance—compared to the conventional fluorophores, low cost, biocompatibility, low toxicity, sensitivity to external stimuli, and scalable production, which makes them excellent candidates for bacterial sensing (Baker and Baker, 2010; Cui et al., 2020; Hola et al., 2014; Lim et al., 2015). Apart from CNPs, metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles are other classes of NPs that possess unique properties such as surface plasmon resonance (SPR), making them a potential candidate for detecting/imaging bacterial pathogens (Peng and Chen, 2019; Zhao et al., 2018). Intense attention has been granted to the development of nanoprobes based on local SPR. Utilizing SPR enables the development of colorimetric, fluorescent, and nonlinear optical sensors for bacterial detection (Zhao et al., 2018).

Many technologies have been introduced in the area of bacterial detection by utilizing nanoparticles as a sensing nanoprobe. Diagnosing the bacterial infection is vital for controlling their outbreak and for preventing the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. To this end, it is essential to understand the fundamental mechanisms and concepts behind microorganism detection using nanoparticles, which will eventually lead to the generation of sensitive nanoprobe for bacterial detection. In this review, we will discuss the recent advances in the development of NPs-based microorganism sensing platforms, providing a solution to one of the urgent medical challenges—pathogenic bacterial detection. We will first review the approaches utilizing different NPs as a nanoprobe for bacterial sensing. Then we will discuss in great detail the applications of the NPs (i.e., CNPs, metallic and a metal oxide, etc.) and nanocomposites in bacterial sensing within various body-fluid samples or in vivo. We will also be discussing the advantages behind the use of NPs in the microorganism identification and how their physicochemical properties such as size, surface charge, morphology, and surface modification affect their bacterial detection ability. The review also provides a perspective to further improve current systems or designing approaches to develop a new generation of nano-sensors. Finally, we are discussing the prospects of NPs-based biosensor and nanoprobe and the associated challenges with such probes in diagnosing infectious bacterial diseases in clinics.

It has been established that scaling down the materials to the nanoscopic sizes affect their optical, electrical, mechanical, and magnetic properties (Asokan, 2019; Stewart et al., 2019). Nanoparticles can be defined as particles that present at the nanometer level having dimensions less than 100 nm. Nanomaterials are appealing to the researchers in the scientific field and biology because they exhibit unique physical properties such as conductivity, uniformity, unique optical properties, and large surface area to volume ratio, thus allowing a more substantial space for sensing and recognition. Further, the ability to modify them easily—their inherent fluorescence—such as carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) and their excellent biocompatibility, making them potential candidates for nanoprobes. Simultaneously the ability of the nanoparticles to be used for accelerating signal transduction provide them a high-throughput sensing platform with enhanced signal intensity. Scheme 1 depicts the various types of NPs and the sensing approaches used for bacterial sensing.

Carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) or carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of nanomaterials that exhibit inherent optical fluorescence. The structure of CNPs consists of amorphous carbon core with graphitic or turbostratic sp2 hybridization (Fathi et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019b). CNPs have abundant hydrophilic functional groups on their quasi-spherical surface. These surface functional groups, like carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups found to affect the fluorescence properties of CNPs (Bhattacharya et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2016; Gong et al., 2019). The functional groups on the surface of CNPs responsible for their unique response to the environment and their excellent biocompatibility. Fig. 1 a illustrates the role of functional groups in shifting the fluorescence emission, which can occur as a response to the time-dependent oxidation of the small molecules passivated on the surface of CDs (Srivastava et al., 2018). Fig. 1b depicts the tuning of the CNPs fluorescence emission by altering the degree of their surface oxidation (for example, having abundant –COOH functional groups) (Ding et al., 2016).

The as-synthesized CNPs have strong absorption in the UV range (260–320 nm), with an extended shoulder in the visible spectrum. This sharp absorption peak is due to the π–π* transition of the aromatic C–C bonds, while the shoulder is usually due to the n–π* transition of the CO bonds (Zhu et al., 2019). Fig. 1c illustrates a representative band-gap model of the CNPs.

The unique optical properties of CNPs allow tuning of their emission-peak location and fluorescence intensity. This can be achieved by either exciting the CNPs at multiple wavelengths or by applying external stimuli such as pH, temperature, or the presence of the specific analytes (Alafeef et al., 2019a; Misra et al., 2017; Pandit et al., 2019; Srivastava et al. 2017, 2019). The synthesis of CNPs can be done in several ways, which can be categorized into either top-down or bottom-up methods, as shown in Fig. 2 .

The emergence of CNPs as universal luminophores is, therefore, owe to their engineerable surface chemistry, excellent photostability, biocompatibility, resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, facile synthetic strategies in large scale, and tunable optical properties. Further, the photoluminescence properties of CNPs are strongly dependent on the presence of specific analytes (Majumdar et al., 2016), pH of the medium (Zhang et al., 2019), or presence of proteins (Freire et al., 2018) in the surrounding microenvironment. To this end, CNPs represent a potential candidate for biosensing applications, which have seen an enormous expansion in their use as a biosensor. This is also partly due to the recent developments of nano-synthetic methodologies, which deepens the understanding of the optical properties of CNPs (Sun and Lei, 2017). But this also should be noted that the application of CNPs were not limited only to biosensing applications, but exceed it to drug and gene delivery (Cao et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017), as well as cell imaging applications (Misra et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2013).

Several detection approaches and methodologies have been employed to detect bacteria using CNPs as a sensing platform. It is worth mentioning here that the fluorescence-based sensing approach is quite sensitive with a high limit of detection (LOD), which can reach even a single-cell level. Therefore the fluorescent biosensors have been widely developed for biomedical diagnostics and bioimaging applications, in which CNPs have been extensively studied (Gunjal et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2017). Bacteria change their environmental pH during the growth process. Upon deposition of CNPs which possess an optical response to pH, a strong interaction between the abundant surface functional groups and the microorganism environment leads to a change in their fluorescence intensity (Basu and Mandal, 2019). By utilizing the pH-dependent fluorescence emission property of the CNPs, Chandra et al. reported a bacterium sensing platform where the CNPs were synthesized via the hydrothermal process using Agaricus bisporus as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen dopant. The as-synthesized CNPs were encapsulated with the microorganism in a microgel structure (Fig. 3 a), and their fluorescence emission was recorded over time to monitor the growth of E. coli within the tested sample. During the microorganism growth, a noticeable change in the fluorescence intensity of the CNPs was observed within 4–6 h, with LOD of ~104 cells (Fig. 3b). The change of the fluorescence signal was attributed to the time-dependent change in the pH of the microorganism environment (Fig. 3c–d).

Employing the phenomenon of pH-dependent aggregation-induced emission quenching of CNPs, Alafeef et al. used Yttrium doped carbon nanoparticles (YCNPs), made from Tannic acid as the carbon source, to selectively detect different types of bacteria with different cell densities. The YCNPs were embedded within a 3D matrix bed made from agarose gel, and the porous nature of the agarose gel enabled the sensor to operate in the solid-state and facilitate the interaction between the bacteria and CNPs. In this work, the output emission was used to determine three primary outcomes. First, determination of the number of bacterial cells within the sample with LOD reaching a single-cell level; Second, identifying the type of bacteria selectively with almost 100% accuracy by utilizing a machine-learning algorithm known as K-mean clustering; and third, monitoring the bacterial growth over time (5 h). The pH responsiveness of the YCNPs was thus attributed to both the deportation of the surface functional groups at high pH and the pH aggregation-induced quenching of the particles, as shown in Fig. 4 . In this work, the developed platform was able to detect the pathogen selectively up to a single-cell resolution, therefore, provides a promising label-free approach for bacterial detection (Alafeef et al., 2019a). Simultaneously, researchers have modified the surface functionalities of CNPs extensively to bring their selectivity towards various bacteria. CNPs functionalized with vancomycin showed their selectivity towards the Gram-positive bacteria in lieu of the ligand-receptor interactions between vancomycin and the bacterial cell wall (Zhong et al., 2015). Colistin (Chandra et al., 2017) and mannose (Lai et al., 2016) were also used for selectivity towards E. coli, and thus modified CNPs were developed for the selective detection of this bacteria. In a separate approach, papaya powder was used to generate green fluorescent CNPs, which were demonstrated for the selective detection of E. coli O157: H7 detection, with a LOD of 9.5 × 104 CFU/mL (Wang et al., 2016c).

The surface chemistry of the CNPs can thus be engineered, and their optical properties can be profoundly affected by the precursor and the synthetic conditions, leading to diverse interactions with the microorganisms. Utilizing gram shells as a carbon precursor for the synthesis of CNPs via pyrolysis, Das et al. showed that the CNPs adhere to the bacterial cell wall, leading to changes in their photoluminescence (PL) (Das et al., 2017). Therefore, these particles can be used to label the bacterial pathogens, which also facilitates their examination under the microscope. Synthesizing CNPs with varying amphiphilic functional groups, Nandi et al. harnessed the excitation-wavelength-dependent fluorescence of CNPs for image-multiplexing of bacteria. These phenomena arise from the fact that the emission spectra can be tuned by exciting the CNPs at various wavelengths (Nandi et al., 2015). Therefore, the use of several excitation channels can provide a wealth of information about bacteria labeling and thus can be visualized easily under the microscope. Taking the advantages of the inherent optical responsiveness of the CNPs to the presence of bacterial pathogens, a cost-effective platform for pathogen detection can be designed without the need for additional modification steps. Three different CNPs functionalized with vancomycin, boronic acid, and polymixin were developed and used in a similar manner for the fluorometric detection of various bacteria (Zheng et al., 2019). The sensor binds with all the different microbial surfaces in various binding affinity, and their relative change in fluorescence intensities provided a distinct molecular fingerprint for the microbial. In addition, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was utilized to detect bacterial presence in the surrounding media (Choi et al., 2018). Here, the fluorescence of the diol modified probe (DYE) was initially quenched by the phenylboronic acid-functionalized CNPs, which was restored in the presence of a bacterium containing glucose on its surface.

Apart from the change in the fluorescence due to the interaction between the surface groups of the non-passivated CNPs and the microorganism, the bacterial interaction with the CNPs by ligand-receptor interaction has also been utilized to improve the selectivity and specificity. To this end, immunofluorescence based approaches have been used by Yang et al. in which carbon dots (CDs) encapsulated breakable organosilica nanocapsules (BONs) has been used for Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) detection (Yang et al., 2018). In this work, immunomagnetic nanoparticles with anti-S. aureus antibodies were utilized to selectively immobilize the targeted bacterial cell, whereas an immunofluorescence nanoparticle conjugated with anti-S.aureus antibody was used as an optical reporter. The immunofluorescence nanoparticles of hundreds of CDs were encapsulated in each nanocapsule. NaBH4 was used as a releasing agent beyond the binding of the nanocapsule to the bacterial cells. By this approach, S. aureus was selectively identified in the range of 1–200 CFUs/mL (Yang et al., 2018). Further, Wang et al. utilized aptamer-conjugated carbon dots for the detection of Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium) by recording their fluorescence signal (Wang et al., 2015). Here, selective labeling and detection of the microorganism have been achieved by the aptamer-conjugated fluorescence nanoparticles, thus, circumventing the limitation of label-free bacterial labeling. However, we are compromising with the system's affordability, multiple processing steps, as well as the complexity of the developed platform. Therefore, compensation between the cost, simplicity, detection sensitivity and the specificity should be made to develop an optimal bacterial detection system.

Localized surface plasmon resonance (SPR) is a unique optical property of the metallic NPs. The incident light can stimulate a resonant oscillation of conduction electrons located at the surface of metallic nanoparticles, which is known as SPR (Haes and Van Duyne, 2004; Rycenga et al., 2011). This phenomenon granted great interest and has been studied extensively. The interparticle interactions and the dielectric properties of the surrounding environment together with the size, shape, and composition of the NPs found to affect the SPR of the metallic nanostructures (Aravind and Metiu, 1980; Chen et al., 2008; Ghosh and Pal, 2007; Miller et al., 2015). SPR of the metallic NPs span a wide spectral range, which can be tuned from ultraviolet to the near-infrared (NIR) region. One SPR band in the visible region was observed in the case of Au nanocubes and nanospheres, whereas multiple SPR bands are founds in other Au nanostructures such as nanorods, nanoclusters (Li et al., 2019a), nanobranches, nano-bipyramids, and nanobeacons (Pan et al., 2010). For example, Au nanorods possess both transverse and longitudinal SPR band. The transverse SPR band is in the visible range, whereas the aspect ratio of rods controls the longitudinal band ranging from visible to NIR range (Chen et al., 2008). Remarkable local electromagnetic field amplification has been observed when the incident light is in resonance with the SPR band of the NPs, (Halas et al., 2011), which consider as the basic principle behind the plasmon-enhanced spectroscopies (Kinkhabwala et al., 2009; Lim et al., 2009). Enormous advancements have been seen in this field due to the discovery of SPR effects of metallic NPs and the improvement in nanofabrication technology. Therefore, the plasmonic of the metallic NPs play a critical role in biosensing (Misra et al., 2018; Yuan et al., 2018b), drug and gene delivery (Mieszawska et al., 2013), imaging (Mangadlao et al., 2018), and ablation therapy (Grosges and Barchiesi, 2018), making the metallic NPs an ideal candidate for designing various biological detection and imaging platforms.

The first demonstration of the use of metallic NPs as a colorimetric indicator was introduced by Mirkin et al., in 1996. DNA has been detected using gold NPs as a colorimetric recognition element (Mirkin et al., 1996). Target-induced color change has been observed from this colorimetric indicator. These changes can be seen by the naked eye or quantitatively recorded by UV–Vis spectrometry. Metallic NPs generally exhibits a high extinction coefficient that outdoes the conventional dyes (Haes and Van Duyne, 2004). Therefore, the change in color as a response to the target can be easily visualized by naked eye. Gold and silver nanoparticles show a change in color due to the aggregation of the NPs. The suspension of silver nanoparticles exhibits a color that varies from yellow to brown and from red to purple in the case of AuNPs. This dramatic color change can be easily observed without the need for complex instruments. Therefore, the colorimetric sensing found lots of applications in biological detection, such as for nucleic acids, enzymes, pathogens, and small molecules (Huang et al., 2017; Ray et al., 2012).

The visual method, i.e., colorimetric detection of pathogens, is an important tool for real-time application and metal nanoparticles, namely gold and silver, as a class of anisotropic plasmonic nanostructures can greatly benefit to this development (Li et al., 2019b; Saha et al., 2012; Zeng et al., 2020). Two of the components are required for any functional sensors for their activity: (i) recognition site for the target analyte to bind with the receptor nanoparticles and (ii) the signal transducer to exhibit the analyte binding. The binding mechanism behind the colorimetric response, therefore, lies in the manipulation of interparticle distance, orientation (spatial arrangement), morphology, size, and composition of the plasmonic nanostructures with the addition of target analyte pathogenic bacteria (Peng et al., 2018; Saha et al., 2012; Zeng et al., 2020). This will induce a distinguishable change in the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), UV–Visible absorbance spectra, and color of the solution containing the plasmonic nanostructures, i.e., metal nanoparticles. Upon binding with the target analyte, the increase in the size of the AuNPs leads to a decrease in its discrete energy gap, an increase in the number of conduction electrons, and a change in LSPR absorption wavelength. Further, the aggregation of AuNPs decreases the inter-particle distance and changes the inter-particle plasmon coupling leading to the redshift in their absorbance spectra. Thus, the colorimetric detection strategies are broadly classified into six types, i.e., analyte induced growth, aggregation, assembly, transformation, etching, and orientation, depending upon the underlying mechanism of interaction between the metal nanoparticles and the target analyte bacteria and the change in the local chemical environment.

Gold (Au) are commonly used as colorimetric recognition elements for sensing platforms because of their intrinsic advantages such as controllable synthesis, easy-to-modify, biocompatibility, and good dispersibility. Now, the abundance of teichoic acids and lipopolysaccharides on Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, respectively, provides the bacterial cells their negative charge. Therefore, positively charged Au NPs electrostatically adhere to the bacterial surface leading to the aggregation of Au NPs on the bacterial surface, resulting in the formation of large entities of Au NPs (Berry et al., 2005; Hayden et al., 2012). As a result of this, an aggregation-induced color change was observed, which selectively depend on the bacterial type (Li et al., 2015a). Peng et al. reported another version of the colorimetric platform using Au NPs. Chimeric phages (viruses) have been used as a recognition element to obtain the selectivity in bacterial detection (Fig. 5 ) owing to the specific interaction between the bacteria and the phages (Peng and Chen, 2019). Phage M13 was engineered to emerge receptor binding protein from a phage that naturally targets particular bacteria. The phages then thiolate to bind to Au-NPs, which act as signal amplification by selective aggregates when the phages bind to the target bacterial species. Shortening the separation distance between the Au NPs leads to plasmon coupling between the particles, resulting in a distinct color change in the solution. Also, the potential of the use of engineered phage-conjugated Au NPs to selectively identify multiple strains (human and plant pathogens) has been explored. Both the targeted bacteria in seawater and human serum have been successfully detected by this colorimetric assay with a limit of detection of 102 CFU. In a separate approach, (Du et al., 2020), flower-shaped Au NPs were used as a colorimetric sensor and assisted with a multiplex PCR assay for rapid and simultaneous detection of S. typhimurium, L. monocytogenes, and E. coli O157:H7, which are the most pathogenic foodborne bacteria. Meanwhile, an adhesion peptide (Chen et al., 2018a) (WGLHTSATNLYLHGGGC, P937), designed by the phage display biopanning technology and having high binding affinity for bacteria, was conjugated with Au nanorods (Au NRs) and utilized for the detection of E. coli and S. aureus at a minimal concentration using the sensitive longitudinal surface plasmon resonance response of the peptide modified Au nanorods. Meanwhile, a unique bacteria-instructed click chemistry approach has been demonstrated to metabolically reduce exogenous copper (II) to copper(I) and act as a catalyst to initiate the click reaction between the modified Au NPs with azide and alkyne functional moieties. This leads to the aggregation of the functionalized Au NPs with a change in color and thus this strategy was used as a colorimetric sensor for point-of-care detection of pathogenic bacteria. This sensing platform (Mou et al., 2019) was also integrated in a smartphone app for the rapid detection of E. coli with high sensitivity (40 CFU/mL) even in complex artificial sepsis blood samples.

In addition to the colorimetric methods, bacterial detection using metallic nanoparticles by fluorescence-based methods have been reported. Utilizing the fluorescence as a readout signal provides excellent sensitivity and better LOD, which can even reach the single-cell resolution. Panda et al. reported a simple and rapid assay to determine the number of bacterial cells using AuNP–polythiophene nanocomposite (Panda et al., 2008). The cell number of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria could be quantitatively identified by recording the fluorescence intensity of the NPs in the presence of bacterial cells with a limit of detection of 103 cells.

Apart from the fluorophore-conjugated Au NPs, the SPR effect on the optical properties of fluorophores has also been studied and further employed for bacterial detection. The excitation of the SPR of noble metal NPs causes a substantial enhancement in the field near the NP surface, which is originated from the extreme field confinement (Guan et al., 2013; Halas et al., 2011). Therefore, it significantly changes the optical properties of the nearby fluorophores' due to the interaction with the metallic NPs (Halas et al., 2011; Huang et al., 2017; LEE et al., 2010; Yuan et al., 2012). The fluorescence may enhance or quench depending on the separation distance between the metal and fluorophores. One of the most investigated phenomena in the field of biosensors is, therefore, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-induced fluorescence quenching. In such a platform, the fluorescence of fluorophores can be effectively quenched by metal NPs in the presence/absence of the bacteria. Au NPs are the most commonly used fluorescence quenchers owing to their strong fluorescence quenching capability due to their large molar extinction coefficients and tunable extinction spectra (Huang et al., 2017). The proximity of the fluorophores from the surface of Au NPs leads to the quenching of emission through the nano-surface energy transfer phenomenon (NSET) (Kumar et al., 2020). This phenomenon of Au NP induced fluorescence quenching has been utilized in numerous biosensing applications. Two strategies are mainly used in these biosensors – target-induced fluorescence quenching and target induced fluorescence recovery. For the target-induced fluorescence quenching, in the absence of a target, the fluorophores are far away from Au NPs, resulting in a strong fluorescence. The separation distance between fluorophores and Au NPs diminishes, in the presence of a target analyst, causing a decrease of fluorescence emission because of FRET (Yu et al., 2017). Although the target-induced fluorescence quenching approach is effective in the detection of the targeted analyte, however, this approach suffers from the background noise caused by strong fluorescence in the absence of target. On another hand, the target-induced fluorescence recovery method can efficiently minimize or eliminate the background noise. In this approach, in the absence of the target, fluorescence is quenched by Au NPs by forming the complex between fluorophores and Au NPs. In the presence of a target, the fluorescence is recovered by detaching the fluorophores from the Au NP surface. Zhang et al. reported a sensitive biosensor assay based on the fluorescence readout for the rapid detection of Salmonella enteritidis (Zhang et al., 2009). In this work, the Au NP is coated with the target-specific DNA probe, which can recognize the target gene, acting as a fluorescence signal quencher. In a separate approach, magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) conjugated with a second target-specific DNA probe has been utilized as a separator and pre-concentrator. In the presence of the target, MNP-Au NP forms a complex which can be separated from the unreacted NPs by a magnetic field. Next, the fluorescence recovery happens by the release of the barcode DNA, in which the fluorescence signal has an exponential relationship with the target concentration. Li et al. reported the design of Au NP-based multichannel fluorescence biosensor for bacterial detection. The sensor was used to detect the bacterial species in the biofilms using the fluorescent proteins conjugated to Au NPs (Li et al., 2014). The disruption of biofilms’ formation by the protein-NPs conjugates exhibits an instantaneous readout represented by three colors. A rapid differentiation of the bacterial species was feasible using the ratiometric response of the readout strains of six different biofilms. Table 1 summarizes the performance of several nanoparticle-based bacterial detection approaches, their response time, and their limit of detection.

Luminescent gold nanoclusters (Au NC) (Goswami et al., 2018) were utilized for the enumeration and detection of bacterial contaminants and strain differentiation. Fluorescent Au NCs (Hossein-Nejad-Ariani et al., 2018) were used to detect L. monocytogenes, a Gram-positive pathogen commonly found in food and pharmaceutical products, which were immobilized on a glass surface coated with a monolayer assembly of leucocin A, an antimicrobial peptide known to have specific interaction against listeria. Vancomycin modified fluorescent Au NCs combined (Cheng et al., 2016) with aptamer and antibiotic based dual recognition approach were also investigated for the selective quantification of S. aureus.

Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) generated from the metallic nanoparticles is another modern technique which was widely utilized for the pathogenic bacterial detection. In one such case, buckled PDMS silver nanorod arrays (Kumar et al., 2015) was developed as an active 3D SERS cage for the facile detection of Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa). Here the principle of detection depends on the enhancement of Raman signal generated by the bacteria due to the formation of high density ‘hot spots’ in the silver nanorod arrays. Positively charged Ag/Au bimetallic (Prakash et al., 2020) nanoparticles were also investigated as a potential SERS platform for label-free classification and discrimination of three different bacteria, namely S. typhimurium, E. coli and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) using multivariate analyses like principal component analysis (PCA) and canonical discriminant analysis. Single bacterial biosensing (Zhang et al., 2018) was also achieved based on the SERS signal received from Ag cylindrical nanotrough networks, compiled with PCA, and used for spectral discrimination between E. coli variants E. coli DH 5α and E. coli BL21. Droplet wet-dry critical SERS approach (Qiu et al., 2016) combined with 3D PCA was utilized for the discrimination among the three most pathogenic food-borne Gram-positive bacteria, i.e., L. monocytogenes, Enterococcus faecium and Staphylococcus xylosus, which provide highly analogous Raman spectra.

In a separate approach, the electrochemical response generated from the Au NPs was used for the detection of pathogenic bacteria. Calcium crosslinked pectin modified (Krithiga et al., 2016) Au NPs deposited on glassy carbon electrode was utilized for the immuno-detection of P. aeruginosa in water. Cyclic voltammetry and impedance spectroscopy were used to evaluate the efficacy of the sensor in each step. A novel cytosensor was developed (Sedki et al., 2020) recently based on the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), which detects the Au NPs immobilized with non-lytic M13 phage particles selectively and thus showed to have the potential for the real-time sensing of fecal coliforms.

In another attempt, metal oxide nanoparticles, along with metallic ones, were used for bacterial detection. Recently computed tomography (CT) images were utilized for bacterial detection. Ostadhossein et al. showed that Hafnium-based nanoparticles could highlight the presence of Streptococcus mutans (S. mutans) as a model bacterium by serving as CT contrast agents (Ostadhossein et al., 2018). In this study, hafnium oxide (HfO2) NPs were subjected to surface modification for potential use in the biofilm diagnosis using CT system, leading eventually to the curing of the infection. HfO2 NPs were synthesized, and the radiopacity of HfO2 NPs were demonstrated using the phantom of a human tooth at ex vivo conditions (Fig. 6 ). In addition to the role of HfO2 NPs as a CT contrast agent, it was also demonstrated that these particles could inhibit bacterial activity such as S. mutans, a bacterial species causing dental decay. Landry et al. have also developed nonsolution based zinc oxide (ZnO) nanosensors (Landry et al., 2018) with superior control over their size, morphology, and microbial biosensing capability mediated by the combinatorial use of magnetic fields and the force of gravity. Multianchored glycoconjugate functionalized magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (Raval et al., 2017) were also explored for selective detection followed by the killing of the enterotoxigenic E. coli strain K99 (EC K99) using the alternating magnetic field (AMF). Herein the magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles were prepared in the presence of poly (ethylene oxide)-poly (acrylic acid)-dopamine and functionalized with bacteria-specific glycoconjugate for the selectivity of the modified metal oxide nanoparticles towards EC K99 with minimal damage to the non-targeted bacterial species. A porous nickel made impedance electrode (Wu et al., 2018) biosensor immobilized with zinc-copper oxide (Zn–CuO) nanoparticles, and graphene oxide nanosheets were also demonstrated for the ultrasensitive bacterial detection in blood. A plethora of opportunities were also brought by the development of metal oxide semiconductor (Formisano et al., 2016) field-effect transistors (MOSFETs). The initial rapid screening of pathogens can be achieved by these sensors where the glycosylated gates of a MOSFET were electrochemically bound with the bacterial membrane, which was found to be at least 10,000 times more sensitive than the EIS and MALDI-ToF analyses.

Various kinds of nanocomposites are also being investigated for their eminent selectivity towards different pathogenic bacteria. In this regard, the surface chemistry and design of the nanocomposites were suitably varied for their optimum selectivity towards the microbial species. A sensor array (Yang et al., 2020) was developed based on a unique nanocomposite, made from positively charged cetyltrimethylammonium bromide capped gold nanoparticles, and negatively charged vancomycin tethered gold nanoclusters, for the selective detection of at least ten different types of Gram-negative bacteria including three kinds of antibiotic-resistant strains. The sensor array was successfully applied to discriminate bacteria at a low detection limit even in clinical settings, as in human urine, utilizing the triple optical patterns of Gram-negative bacteria with the assistance of linear discriminant analysis (LDA). In a separate approach (Bonnet et al., 2018), an electrochemical sandwich immunoassay was developed for E. coli detection in platelet concentrates. Herein the assay was designed as a temperature-sensitive nano-tool where the magnetic beads were specifically modified with antibodies to target the bacteria, and the methylene blue-DNA/nanoparticle assemblies were utilized to receive electrochemical response leading towards the amplified bacterial detection. A unique nanocomposite made up of multiwalled carbon nanotubes–chitosan–bismuth complex (Fernandes et al., 2015) and lead sulfide nanoparticles was reported for the electrochemical detection of pathogenic Aeromonas. The two-oligonucleotide modified signaling probe and fixing probe-target the Aeromonas DNA sequence selectively, which was detected by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) generated from the released lead sulfide nanoparticles. In a different approach, zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 was formed (Zhong et al., 2019), and cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs) were encapsulated inside the metal-organic framework to generate a multi-core shell nanoparticle for the ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of E. coli O157:H7. The particles were coated with polyethyleneimine to introduce amino functional groups, which were further modified with anti-E. coli O157:H7 antibody. The differential pulse voltammetric signals were successfully analyzed to detect the bacterial presence even in milk samples. Separately, mannosylated cellulose nanofibers (Cauwel et al., 2019) and cellulose papers were investigated for their strong binding affinity towards the FimH lectin leading towards the selective discrimination of pathogenic E. coli strains from non-pathogenic ones. DNA nanopyramids (Giovanni et al., 2015) anchor was also utilized for the immunological detection of E. coli lipopolysaccharides, lysate, and whole bacteria without any interference from several other macromolecules. This technology was thus tested with a potential for the quantification of E. coli content in real water samples. A nanocomposite (Han et al., 2017), made from the negatively charged poly (para-phenylene ethynylene) (PPE) and four different positively charged antimicrobial peptides (AMP), was investigated for the discrimination of fourteen different bacteria in water and in human urine by pattern-based fluorescence recognition associated with linear discriminant analysis (LDA). Here the fluorescence of PPE was partially quenched by the AMPs, and the AMP-PPE complexes were then used to differentially bind with the constituents of bacterial surfaces. An electrochemical immunosensor (Gayathri et al., 2016) was developed for the efficacious detection of uropathogenic E. coli in water where thionine dye immobilized on multi-walled carbon nanotube and chitosan made nanocomposite was coated on glassy carbon electrode. Brush-like magnetic nanoprobes (Yang et al., 2017a), suitably functionalized with poly-L-lysine (PLL) followed by coupling with carboxylated polyethyleneglycol (PEG) to the amine sites of PLL, were utilized for the conjugation of vancomycin with the carboxyl of the PEG. This nanocomposite was then demonstrated for the electrochemiluminescence detection of L. monocytogenes in clinical samples.

In a different approach, (Han et al., 2019), magnetic core-shell nanocomposite (Fe3O4@SiO2–Pt) was demonstrated as a multifunctional platform for excellent peroxide enzyme activity and selective identification of pathogenic bacteria. In a separate study (Liao et al., 2019), metal-assisted chemical etching was utilized to fabricate vertically aligned silicon nanowire arrays, which were then decorated by uniform Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles. This nanocomposite tested via laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) and label based surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), exhibited efficient capture capacity for bacteria in drinking water, e.g., E. coli and S. aureus within a short period of time. Nevertheless, the SERS technique has been widely exploited for the detection and characterization of different antibiotic-resistant bacteria (Liu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018b). Recently, new chemiluminescent probes (Roth-Konforti et al., 2019), composed of phenoxy-dioxetane luminophore masked by a triggering group, were also reported for the ultrasensitive SERS based detection of viable pathogenic bacteria, namely Salmonella and L. monocytogenes. The trigger could be activated by a specific bacterial enzyme, and the nanocomposite would thus be able to detect bacteria selectively within a short period with a LOD of about 600-fold lower than that of the common fluorescent probes. Separately, magnetic nanoparticles functionalized (Yuan et al., 2018a) with antimicrobial peptides were used as “capturing” probes for bacterial separation and gold coated silver doped graphene oxide (Au@Ag-GO) nanocomposite functionalized with 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid (4-MPBA) was then utilized as SERS tags for specific recognition of bacterial pathogens. Depending on the differential SERS intensities produced from the interaction of 4-MPBA and bacterial cell membrane, three bacterial pathogens, namely E. coli, S. aureus, and P. aeruginosa were successfully discriminated at an ultralow detection limit even from clinical samples.

The polymerization of functional monomers with crosslinking agents in the presence of a template molecule generates the molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs). A cavity is generated when the template is removed from the scaffold leading to the physical and mechanical stability of the MIPs along with their selectivity towards an analyte (Cao et al., 2019; Pirzada and Altintas, 2019). Computational studies were widely utilized in the selection of templates when MIPs were used for the selective detection of pathogenic bacteria. In one such case, Altintas et al. used itaconic acid as the functional monomer for the ultrasensitive detection of E. coli endotoxins (Altintas et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2018). Van Grinsven and co-workers have undertaken surface molecular imprinted (SMI) polymers (Ertürk and Mattiasson, 2017) to receive the change in thermal resistance followed by selective detection of E. coli and S. aureus strains (van Grinsven et al., 2016). The same group also utilized thermal wave transport analysis along with SMI polymers to reduce the noise originated from the thermal resistance signal and improved the LOD for bacterial detection (Steen Redeker et al., 2017). In another approach, various nano-polymers are also used for the detection of pathogenic bacteria (Carvalho et al., 2018). In one such case, Robby and Park synthesized a nanocomposite of polymer dots and intercalated it into montmorillonite to visually detect a broad range of E. coli and S. aureus concentration (Robby and Park, 2019). Schonherr and co-workers used chitosan hydrogels embedded with the fluorogenic and chromogenic substrate as a self-reporting platform for the detection of the β-glucuronidase enzyme that is secreted by most of the known E. coli strains (Sadat Ebrahimi et al., 2015). Sheikhzadeh et al. utilized conducting aptamer conjugated polymer to develop an impedimetric biosensor for the selective detection of S. typhimurium with good reproducibility and lower LOD and LOQ values (Sheikhzadeh et al., 2016). The characteristic features of cationic polymers to differentially interact with the bacterial cell membrane via electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions are also utilized for the pathogenic bacterial sensing applications. In this regard, Wang et al. used cationic conjugated poly (fluorine-co-phenylene) (PFP–NMe3 +) polymer modified with cucurbit [7]uril and monitored the change in the fluorescence emission intensity upon its interaction with different bacterial surfaces. The specific detection of different strains of Gram-negative (E. coli, P. aeruginosa) and Gram-positive (B. subtilis, S. aureus, Enterococcus faecalis (E. faecalis)) bacteria was demonstrated (Bai et al., 2016). Poly (vinylpyrrolidone) quaternized with ethyl bromide and modified with the fluorophore, boron dipyrromethane (BODIPY), was also undertaken to detect E. coli and S. aureus in solution (Islamy Mazrad et al., 2017).

Each year, more than 2 million people are killed worldwide by pathogen-related diseases such as sepsis and endocarditis (Levy and Marshall, 2004; Li and Webster, 2018; Panizzi et al., 2011). Diagnosis and curing of bacterial infection in vivo are, therefore, with high demand to eliminate them in early stages. Nanoparticles-based cure and diagnostic strategy are promising for pathogen detection in vivo owing to their high specificity, sensitivity, and efficiency in pathogen identification and curing (Ning et al., 2011; Norouz Dizaji et al., 2019; Ostadhossein et al., 2018). To achieve high specificity, NPs should be selectively uptake by the bacterial cells but not the mammalian cells, therefore, avoid the false-positive error. Surface modified nanoparticles with a glucose polymer are one approach to ensure the selective internalization of the NPs by the bacterial cell membrane. Glucose polymer (GP) such as poly [4-O-(α-D-glucopyranosyl)-D-glucopyranose] has been utilized by Tang et al. to develop a multifunctional nanoagents for both imaging and curing bacterial infections (Tang et al., 2019). The use of this carbon source enables the nanoparticles to internalize the bacterial membrane selectively, and not the mammalian cells. The GP internalization occurs at a high uptake rate through the transporter known as GP-mediated ATP-binding cassette (ABC) present on the pathogenic bacterial membrane (Boos and Shuman, 1998; Bordignon et al., 2010; Charbit, 2003; Dippel and Boos, 2005; Gopal et al., 2010; Klebba, 2002). On the other hand, it is difficult for GP to internalize the mammalian cells because the mammalian cells lack the GP-mediated ABC transporters (Boos and Shuman, 1998; Freundlieb et al., 1988). The surface of fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) have been modified with the glucose polymer—poly [4-O-(α-D-glucopyranosyl)-D-glucopyranose]— and used as an optical reporter. For bacterial pathogen curing, the as-prepared SiNPs was loaded with chlorin e6 (Ce6), which generate reactive oxygen species when irradiated with a 660 nm laser. Further, Ce6 also serves as a second optical reporter (670 nm) for bacterial imaging in vivo (Ding et al., 2018; Feng et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018a). This approach was successful in imaging bacteria in vivo of as low as 105 CFUs. Further, the nano-composition mentioned above shows in vivo photodynamic antibacterial activities in which about 98% S. aureus and 96% P. aeruginosa has been cured. A heterodimer of up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) (Sun et al., 2018) and gold yolk-shell nanoparticles were monitored through circular dichroism and up-conversion luminescence for their selectivity against polymyxin-B-resistant E. coli. The differential affinity of polymyxin B towards the sensitive and resistant bacteria was utilized herein to receive differential signals from the polymyxin B coupled UCNPs. This dual-mode heterodimer thus demonstrated to have the unique capability for surveillance and control of drug-resistant bacteria in vivo.

Recently, Qing, G. et al.(Qing et al., 2019) developed a synergistic antibacterial strategy that utilizes the advantages of fluorescence monitoring, physical damage mediated through photothermal therapy, and chemical inhibition induced by antibiotics. The smart nanomaterial (Fig. 7 a), namely TRIDENT (Thermo-Responsive-Inspired Drug-Delivery Nano-Transporter), was demonstrated for high antimicrobial efficacy against both the antibiotic-sensitive bacteria and clinical multidrug-resistant bacteria at a relatively low antibiotic dose. It was observed that the near-infrared irradiation mediated increase in temperature (above 43 °C) melted the nanotransporter via a phase change mechanism and irreversibly damaged the bacterial membrane selectively to facilitate the permeabilization of antibiotics. Both the in vitro and in vivo eradication of clinical methicillin-resistant S. aureus was demonstrated (Fig. 7b). A point-of-care diagnostic tool (Guo et al., 2019) was also developed for the recognition of P. aeruginosa and S. aureus directly from a skin burn wound utilizing densely packed gold nanostars as the SERS substrate. Here the gold nanostars were packed between two layers of graphene and functionalized with a synthetic o-nitrobenzyl derivative to generate an artificial bio-interface for the efficient capture of bacteria mediated by the electrostatic interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: New advents of bio-engineering based approaches for bacterial detection
ce:section.ce:para: With the advent of newer technologies, scientists have come up with many different advanced technological improvements for the selective detection of bacteria (Furst and Francis, 2019; Khemthongcharoen et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2020a). In this regard, Etayash et al. have (Etayash et al., 2016) developed a biomaterial microcantilever with an embedded microfluidic channel (Fig. 8 ) for accurate, real-time and high-throughput detection of drug-resistant bacteria. The internal surfaces of the microfluidic channel were either chemically or physically functionalized with specific receptors for bacteria. The adsorption of bacteria inside the cantilever resulted in a decrease in cantilever's resonant frequency, and the excitation of the entrapped bacteria by infrared radiation (IR) caused the cantilever to deflect in proportion to the IR absorption of the bacteria leading to the generation of a nanomechanical IR spectrum selective for in situ detection and discrimination of L. monocytogenes even at a concentration as low as single-cell per mL. Separately, an impedance-based microfluidic biosensor (Liu et al., 2019a), functionalized with antibodies specific for Salmonella serotypes B and D, was developed for selective and rapid detection of Salmonella over E. coli O157 and E. coli DH5-α. A technology derived from phage (Anany et al., 2018) based bioactive paper, made by piezoelectric inkjet printing, was also developed for the selective detection of various foodborne bacterial pathogens, including Escherichia coli O157:H7, E. coli O45:H2, and Salmonella Newport. A disposable all-printed electronic biosensor (Ali et al., 2018), consisting of silver nanowires uniformly fabricated on the electrodes, was also developed for the classification of three of the major pathogenic bacteria, e.g., S. typhimurium, and E. coli strains JM109 and DH5-α. Here the inter-digitated silver electrodes were decorated through a unique electrohydrodynamic technique on top of a polyamide-based polyethylene terephthalate substrate. Researchers have thus developed many advanced technologies including but not limited to microcavity in-line optical fiber based Mach-Zehnder interferometer (Janik et al., 2018), screen-printed graphene electrodes (Kampeera et al., 2019) combined with loop-mediated isothermal amplification, 3D imaging of curved (Veli and Ozcan, 2018) surfaces of contact lenses combined with machine learning algorithm and impedimetric sensing (Wu et al., 2019) through one step electro-polymerization for the label-free biosensing of many pathogenic bacteria like E. coli, Vibrio parahaemolyticus, S. aureus, and E. coli O157:H7 respectively.

It has been established that nanoparticles hold a great promise for developing sensors for bacterial detection. Microorganism sensing can take place both in a laboratory setting by testing bodily fluids or in vivo by tracking the microorganism within the body in real-time (Butler et al., 2019; Choudhary et al., 2017; He et al., 2020; Hicks et al., 2019; Hoerr et al., 2013; Lee et al., 2020; McConnell et al., 2020; Narang et al., 2018; Singh et al., 2019; Trinh et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020). Starting with the POC system, specific requirements should be satisfied by the sensors including selectivity, specificity, low LOD, repeatability, high sensitivity, easy-to-use, and its cost-effectiveness. These sensors vary based on the type of nanoparticles used in the sensor and its output signal. However, they commonly consist of two components: the recognition element and the reporter. The recognition element is responsible for the selective identification of a pathogen, due to the distinct interaction with the microorganism. The recognition element could be an antibody (Khashayar et al., 2017) aptamer-conjugated to the NPs’ surface (Medley et al., 2011) or the nanoparticle itself (Alafeef et al., 2019a). The reporter is the element that provides the read-out signal. These read-out signals include but are not limited to optical, magnetic, electrical, and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) (Galvan and Yu, 2018). Nanoparticles can serve as both the recognition element and the reporter in a label-free approach where it can help as a reporter when the surface is conjugated with a recognition element.

The widespread of wireless internet enables the communication of personal health information wirelessly (Han et al., 2018) and throughout smartphones (Alafeef, 2017; Grossi, 2019). Indeed, the Internet of Things (IoT) plays an essential role in real-time pathogen detection and monitoring using a smartphone and also share data with other points via internet server. The future for the IoT is going toward the Internet of Nano-Things (IoNT), in which nanoscale devices are interconnected and connects to the IoT (Akyildiz and Jornet, 2010; Balasubramaniam and Kangasharju, 2013; Nayyar et al., 2017).

Several combinations of nanomaterial-based platforms like immunosensor (Seo et al., 2016) and others have been developed and combined with IoT for the detection of bacterial pathogens such as MRSA and C. difficile for use in hospital and community settings (i-sense, 2020) to speed up information exchange. IoNT can also be integrated with a flexible sensor for real-time monitoring of tooth bacteria (Mannoor et al., 2012). In addition to the wireless-enabled sensor, the device can be further advanced to be self-powered (Hajizadegan et al., 2017) and smart by incorporating artificial intelligence (Verma and Yadava, 2015).

The combination of artificial intelligence—a subtype of machine-learning (Abdulhay et al., 2018; Alafeef et al., 2019a, 2019b; Alafeef and Fraiwan 2019; Hadoush et al., 2019)—and nanotechnology paving the way of the advancement in the detection approaches of the microorganism and work closely towards the human dream of having a sensor that fully meets all of the aforementioned criteria.

Fig. 9 illustrates a generalized representation of the component of a point-of-care (POC) device for microorganism detection. Mimicking the human perception and decision-making ability through the utilization of machine learning approaches helps improve the sensor outcomes without the necessity of further improvement of the detection aspect. Furthermore, it utilizes wireless capabilities in line with the current healthcare trend. In other words, this strategy will enable easy access to electronic medical records and telemedicine. Although many advances have been made toward the development of a point-of-care device for microorganism detection, there is still enough space for further improvement. The final product of the microorganism sensor should be sensitive (single-cell resolution is recommended), specific with the ability to selectively identify the pathogen type, rapid, reliable, affordable, and easy-to-use.
